items.title: Merge optimize → develop
items.body:
## Summary

Merges the `optimize` branch (159 commits) into `develop`. Key work streams:

- **Tool routing & discovery**: `search_api`, `recommend_tools`, tags on all 142 tools, description enrichment
- **LLM test suite**: 170+ tests, progressive L1/L2/L3, benchmark tracking, cross-model sweeps
- **Geometry tools**: `create_bar_building`, `create_new_building`, `import_floorspacejs`
- **Generic object access**: `get_object_fields`, `set_object_property`, dynamic `list_model_objects`
- **Measure authoring**: full skill with ReportingMeasure support, `search_wiring_patterns`
- **Response-size guardrails**: `max_results` + filters on all list tools, brief mode
- **Agent guardrails**: anti-loop instructions, tool-bypass prevention
- **HVAC**: FourPipeBeam, CooledBeam terminals, `set_zone_equipment_priority`
- **Stdout suppression fix (issue #42)**: permanent fd redirect replaces racy global middleware
- **CI**: 5-shard test matrix, ~450+ integration tests

### Stdout fix (Fixes #42)

Commit `2395d95` replaces the global FastMCP middleware (which held `os.dup2()` on fd 1 for entire tool calls, causing concurrent response loss) with a one-time fd redirect at startup. C-level stdout (SWIG, Polyhedron, etc.) goes to stderr permanently; Python `sys.stdout` writes JSON-RPC to a private fd. No races, no missed callsites.

## Test plan
- [ ] CI shards 1-5 pass
- [ ] `test_concurrent_tools.py` passes (concurrent slow+fast and fast+fast)
- [ ] `test_complex_model_stdout_purity` passes (Polyhedron leak on 44-zone model)
- [ ] No SWIG or Polyhedron messages in stdout

items.title: Replace SWIG XML with libclang for message payload struct parsing
items.body: * **Tickets addressed:** Closes #1342 * **Review:** By commit * **Merge strategy:** Merge (no squash) ## Description Replaces the SWIG `-xml` step with a `libclang`-based parser (`generatePayloadMetaJson.py`) that produces a JSON description of each `*Payload.h` struct. `generateSWIGModules.py` consumes this JSON to emit `RECORDER_PROPERTY_*` macros as before, but now with accurate C-level type metadata (exact sizes, enum values, array shapes, alignment/padding) instead of relying on SWIG XML internals. The richer metadata also enables two improvements to the generated SWIG bindings for each payload class: - A typed, keyword-only `__init__` with per-field type annotations and docstrings sourced directly from the header comments, replacing the generic `**kwargs` constructor. - Typed `@property` descriptors and a `__fields__` classmethod. These improvements allow for nicer IDE support around payloads: - When constructing a payload, we get all the fields it has, valid inputs for them, and a docstring with the description of the fields: - We get a red squiggly line (and a pop up with what's wrong) if we use the wrong type: - The IDE will show you the description of a field if you hover over it: -------- Commits are organized as: (1) new libclang parser + tests, (2) refactor `generateSWIGModules.py` and update the SWIG templates, (3) wire the new pipeline into CMake. `libclang` (via `pip install libclang`) is added as a dev dependency. The parser bundles minimal stub headers so no system Clang installation is required. ## Verification Unit tests were added in `test_generatePayloadMetaJson.py` covering primitives, multi-word C types, type aliases, 1-D/2-D arrays, enums, pointers, nested structs, incomplete types, and C vs C++ parsing modes. A full CMake build was run to confirm all existing message targets produce identical `RECORDER_PROPERTY_*` output. ## Documentation No existing documentation is invalidated. ## Future work If/when merged, @E-Krantz might consider using the newly generated `json` files in his very cool bsk-ros workflow.

items.title: feat: add coraza_is_response_body_processable() API
items.body:
Exposes the Go engine's `IsResponseBodyProcessable()` through the C API so connectors (nginx, apache) can skip response body processing when `SecResponseBodyAccess` is Off or the response Content-Type doesn't match `SecResponseBodyMimeType`.

Returns 1 if the body should be processed, 0 otherwise. Must be called after `coraza_process_response_headers()`.

Related: corazawaf/coraza-nginx#41 (large response hang) and corazawaf/coraza-nginx#42 (nginx fix that uses this API).

cc @fzipi @jptosso

items.title: Replace DATA_PTR and Data_Get_Struct by SWIG_ConvertPtr
items.body:
Mixing ruby functions and SWIG functions to access Ruby/C++ objects is bad practice. It relies on a particular expectation of how SWIG maps C++ objects to Ruby objects.

This commit changes the code to use SWIG functions only and to not use raw Ruby functions to access SWIG mapped objects.

Also change the flag of ConvertPtr to the SWIG provided constant SWIG_POINTER_DISOWN, to make it more clear, that the object will be deleted by the object it was assigned to.

The compiler deprecation warning is re-enabled, since they do no longer appear when built with SWIG with the following patch:
  https://github.com/swig/swig/pull/3326

Fix type conversions of Array\<FXPoint>, Array\<FXRectangle>, Array\<FXSegment> and Array\<FXArc> to raise an error on type mismatch. This wasn't checked before and could lead to invalid memory access.

items.body:
## Summary

Replaces the verbose two-line FixedArray initialization pattern with the modern single-line static factory method `::Filled(v)`.

**Before:**
```cpp
ImageType::SizeType size;
size.Fill(16);
```

**After:**
```cpp
constexpr auto size = ImageType::SizeType::Filled(16);
```

## Rules applied

- **`constexpr auto`** used when the variable is never subsequently modified (test files with concrete types)
- **`auto`** (without `constexpr`) used for accumulator variables in loop bodies (`.hxx` template implementations)
- **Skipped:** cases where `Fill()` is immediately followed by element-wise overrides (`size[0] = w`), or where `typename` disambiguation in deeply nested template parameter scopes makes the replacement non-trivial

## Files changed

| File | Pattern |
|------|---------|
| `Modules/IO/NRRD/test/itkNrrdLocaleTest.cxx` | `SizeType` + `IndexType` → `constexpr auto` |
| `Modules/Video/BridgeOpenCV/test/itkOpenCVVideoIOTest.cxx` | `PointType` → `constexpr auto` |
| `Modules/Filtering/Path/include/itkChainCodeToFourierSeriesPathFilter.hxx` | `VectorType` accumulators → `auto` |
| `Modules/Filtering/QuadEdgeMeshFiltering/include/itkSmoothingQuadEdgeMeshFilter.hxx` | `VectorType` accumulator → `auto` |

Pure style change — no semantic changes.

Suggested by @N-Dekker in https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITK/pull/6003#discussion_r3033221346

items.title: Take constructor by const-ref for ctypes in C++
items.body:
Fixes substitution failure for this situation:

```
shz::vec2 a{};
const shz::vec2 b{};

shz::vec2 c = a - b;
```

Implicit copy constructor is called on the operator overloads. They might also want to be const-ref but  this should solve the root of it.

> error: call of overloaded ‘vecN(const shz::vec2&)’ is ambiguous

Tries:
> shz::vecN<CRTP, C, R>::vecN(CType)
> shz::vecN<shz::vec2, shz_vec2, 2>::vecN(const shz::vecN<shz::vec2, shz_vec2, 2>&)

feat: add session payments with on-chain Swig enforcement
items.body:
## Summary

This PR implements session payment method support following https://mpp.dev/faq#what-are-sessions for the Solana SDK with on-chain Swig smart swallets, and adds full integration coverage for the session lifecycle.

## What changed

- Added session method support in the SDK client/server surface and exports.
- Implemented `SwigSessionAuthorizer` with on-chain policy validation against Swig role state.
    - Validates delegated session signer and role binding.
    - Validates role program permissions and spend limits from chain data.
    - Supports session metadata returned by wallet adapters (`openTx`, `swigRoleId`, `createdAt`) and enforces TTL safely.
- Updated `BudgetAuthorizer` to be Swig-role-backed and fail closed.
    - `swig` config is now required (`swigAddress`, `swigRoleId`, optional `rpcUrl`).
    - Resolves on-chain Swig role constraints on open and clamps effective limits to on-chain caps.
    - Rejects unknown channels for update/topup/close, so non-open actions cannot bypass open-time role validation.
    - Rejects roles that cannot prove program authorization checks.
- Updated `makeSessionAuthorizer` for `wallet-budget` profile.
    - Requires `swigWallet.swigAddress` and `swigWallet.swigRoleId`.
    - Passes Swig config through to `BudgetAuthorizer`.
- Added a Surfpool mitigation in client charge flow.
    - Appends a memo with challenge reference so rapid identical transfers are unique.
    - Prevents `This transaction has already been processed` failures in local integration runs.

## Tests added and updated

- Added full session integration coverage:
    - auto-open then update flow
    - auto-topup management flow
    - close then reopen flow
    - `swig_session` positive on-chain flow with enforced Swig spend limit
    - `regular_budget` positive on-chain Swig role-limited flow
- Added negative integration coverage:
    - `swig_session` rejects delegated signer not present on-chain
    - `regular_budget` rejects unknown configured Swig role

## Validation

- `npm run typecheck` passed
- `npm test` passed
- `npm run test:integration` passed with Surfpool running

items.title: 🛡️ Sentinel: [Low] Fix buffer overflow vulnerability in SWIG wrappers
items.body:
🚨 **Severity:** Low
💡 **Vulnerability:** Buffer overflow potential via `strcpy` in `BNGcore_wrap.cxx`. Even though a length check was present, `strcpy` is unsafe and flags automated vulnerability scanners.
🎯 **Impact:** Prevents theoretical overflows where the source string might exceed the dynamically tracked buffer bounds, improving the security posture of the C++ and SWIG interface.
🛠️ **Fix:** Replaced `strcpy` calls with length-bounded `strncpy` calls, including explicit null-termination of the last byte in the buffer, across both tracked instances of `BNGcore_wrap.cxx`.
✅ **Verification:** Compiled natively (`cd bng2/Network3 && make -f Makefile.cmake && cmake . && make`) and passed the `validate_examples.pl` test suite.

---

items.title: Bitwuzla: Simplify updating the SWIG patch
items.body:
Hello,

this PR is in response to a comment when we last updated the Bitwuzla bindings:
> We should document the steps for updating the swig-patchfile. :-) Quite some manual work to get right paths.
[link](https://github.com/sosy-lab/java-smt/pull/598#issuecomment-3962927859)

To simplify updating the patch the build process for Bitwuzla has now been split into two separate steps:
* We first generate the new SWIG bindings with `ant bitwuzla-generate-jni`
* We then have a chance to fix any issues with the generated code
* Finally, we run `ant publish-bitwuzla` to automatically generate the updated patch file before then compiling the solver binaries

I've documented the process in `Developers-How-to-Release-into-Ivy.md`, including some hints on how to fix common issues with the generated code

Note that I also switched to using the preinstalled `jdks` from the container: We were already using the preinstalled versions of gmp/mpfr, and this removes two more arguments from the command line

OPE-3175 Fix workflow to use unity or dotnet exclusively
items.body:
Ticket: https://magnopus.atlassian.net/browse/OPE-3175

This PR updates the workflows to:

1. Properly filter artifacts between Unity and dotnet, making the workflows exclusive and independent.

2. Fix linking using a variable instead of hard coding the dllname used by dllimport (needed as __Internal for iOS and visionOS due to the static linking, but as ConnectedSpacesPlatformDotNet for platforms that need dynamic linking).

3. Produce a tarball for Unity (.tgz instead of .zip), which is more standard for Unity import flow.

4. Update release pipeline to use release branch (where source codes generated by SWIG are pushed to and versioned) + Github release page (where the versioned libraries and binaries produced by SWIG are published to, versioned).

5. Generate the .meta files for the source code pushed to the release branch of Unity, because once Unity pulls the source codes it stores them into PackageCache, which is immutable. This means that Unity won't be able to generate the .meta required to compile the scripts, so we produce them via CI.


**Testing**
I tested the whole workflow by using a temporary push trigger for this branch, which triggered the make-release.yml flow. As a result, this flow:

1. Produced the libraries (.tgz for Unity and .zip for DotNet, publishing them onto [this Github release page](https://github.com/magnopus-opensource/connected-spaces-platform-unity/releases/tag/test-0.0.1-23)).
2. Pushed the related versioned code generated by SWIG into the [release/unity](https://github.com/magnopus-opensource/connected-spaces-platform-unity/tree/release/unity) and [release/dotnet](https://github.com/magnopus-opensource/connected-spaces-platform-unity/tree/release/dotnet) branches.

I then tested the import into Unity, which I detailed into our internal documenation.

Updated docs: https://magnopus.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/OL/pages/4191060014/CSP+Unity+interop+Release+process

items.title: Version 1.0.0 of the SDK
items.body:
# Description

This PR introduces the bsk-sdk package which is a self-contained Python wheel that gives external projects everything they need to build Basilisk-compatible SWIG plugins without vendoring a full Basilisk codebase / fork.

## Includes
- Curated headers:  messaging headers needed to implement a SysModel subclass
- Runtime stubs (bsk_arch_min, bsk_runtime_min) for a minimal compiled static libs needed for linking like atmosphereBase
- SWIG interface files and typemaps: `.i` files and architecture/ typemaps for wrapping custom modules
- SWIG ABI pinning:  bsk-sdk pins swig>=4.2.1,<=4.3.1 to match the version BSK is built with. SWIG 4.4.0 bumped SWIG_RUNTIME_VERSION from "4" to "5", which changes the shared type-table capsule name. Plugins compiled against the wrong epoch can't exchange objects with BSK across module boundaries. bsk_add_swig_module() detects this at cmake configure time and aborts with a clear error.
- Eigen3 bundled : headers are included in the wheel so plugin authors don't need a separate Eigen install

# CI
1.  Builds wheels across Linux (x86_64 + aarch64), macOS (arm64), and Windows.
2. Installs the built wheel and builds the included custom-atm-plugin example against it on all three platforms

items.title: fix(setup): add swig/liblgpio-dev deps and fix commented overlay dete…
items.body:
…ction on Trixie

Two fresh-install failures uncovered during Pi Zero 2W bring-up on Debian Trixie (aarch64):

1. The lgpio Python package has no prebuilt aarch64 wheel on PyPI, so pip builds it from source. On a fresh Raspberry Pi OS Lite (Trixie) image the build fails first at the swig codegen step, then at the linker step against liblgpio. Add both as apt dependencies so the wheel builds cleanly.

2. The overlay-add loop used 'grep -qF' which matches any substring, including commented lines like '#dtparam=spi=on'. A fresh Trixie config.txt ships SPI/I2C/UART commented-out by default, so the loop skipped them and left the interfaces disabled. Replace with an anchored regex that distinguishes active / commented / missing and uncomments in place via sed when a commented version exists.

Also note in README that the base Trixie Lite image does not ship with git, so it must be installed before cloning the repo.

items.title: Fix PyInstaller Windows `_ctypes` DLL runtime packaging
items.body:
### Motivation
- Address startup failure `ImportError: DLL load failed while importing _ctypes` observed for Windows onefile builds by ensuring required Python runtime DLLs (notably `libffi`) are bundled into the PyInstaller artifact.
- Improve build reliability by raising the minimum PyInstaller/tooling versions to reduce missing-runtime and hook-resolution regressions.

### Description
- Added `packaging/metroliza_onefile.spec` logic to collect Windows Python runtime DLLs from `<python>/DLLs` and pass them into the `Analysis(..., binaries=...)` parameter so `_ctypes` and similar extensions find their runtime dependencies at startup.
- Updated `requirements-build.txt` to bump `pyinstaller` and `pyinstaller-hooks-contrib` minimum versions to more recent releases.
- Added troubleshooting guidance to `docs/native_build_distribution.md` describing the `_ctypes` DLL failure, expected causes, and checks for Windows packaging.

### Testing
- Compiled the updated spec file to validate syntax with `python -m py_compile packaging/metroliza_onefile.spec`, which completed successfully.
- No unit tests were modified; recommend running a Windows packaging smoke test by building the onefile artifact and launching `dist/metroliza.exe` on a clean Windows VM to confirm the runtime issue is resolved (manual validation / follow-up).

What changed: added Windows runtime DLL collection to the PyInstaller spec, bumped packaging tool minimums, and updated docs.
Why: to prevent missing Python runtime DLLs (e.g. `libffi*.dll`) causing `_ctypes` import failures in packaged Windows executables.
How it was verified: spec file syntax checked via `python -m py_compile` and the change is limited to packaging and docs.
Remaining risks / follow-ups: target machines may still require the Microsoft Visual C++ Redistributable and a full CPython install must be used for builds; validate with a clean Windows VM smoke run and adjust bundled DLL globs if additional runtime files are needed.

items.title: Fix ARM64 ESL build: generate config.m4 before phpize
items.body:
The FreeSWITCH ESL `php/` directory doesn't ship a `config.m4`, which `phpize` requires before it can run — causing the ARM64 build path in `install_esl_extension.sh` to fail with "Cannot find config.m4".

## Changes

- **`install/install_esl_extension.sh`**: In `build_esl_from_source()`, insert a `config.m4` generation step between SWIG binding generation and the `phpize` call. The file is only written if it doesn't already exist.

```sh
if [ ! -f "$PHP_EXT_DIR/config.m4" ]; then
  cat > "$PHP_EXT_DIR/config.m4" <<'EOF'
PHP_ARG_ENABLE(esl, whether to enable ESL support,
[  --enable-esl            Enable ESL support])

if test "$PHP_ESL" != "no"; then
  PHP_NEW_EXTENSION(esl, ESL.cpp, $ext_shared)
fi
EOF
fi
```

The rest of the `phpize` → `configure` → `make` flow is unchanged.

items.title: feat: add pto.tquant op with INT8_SYM/ASYM quantization support
items.body:
Adds `pto.tquant`, a DPS tile op that quantizes an `f32` tile into `i8`/`ui8` using a scaling parameter tile. Implements the pto-isa `TQUANT` intrinsic across all compiler layers.

- New `QuantType` enum attr with two modes:
  - `INT8_SYM` — 2-operand form: `ins(%src, %fp)`, `i8` dst
  - `INT8_ASYM` — 3-operand form: `ins(%src, %fp, %offset)`, `ui8` dst
- Custom assembly format handles the optional `offset` operand
- Verifier enforces mode-operand consistency:
  - `INT8_SYM` rejects offset, requires `i8` dst
  - `INT8_ASYM` requires offset (`f32`), requires `ui8` dst
- A2/A3 verifier enforces row-major layout; A5 is layout-agnostic
- EmitC emits `TQUANT<pto::QuantType::INT8_SYM/ASYM, DstTile, SrcTile, FpTile>(dst, src, fp[, &offset])`

## Test plan

- [x] `ptoas --pto-arch=a3 test/basic/tquant.pto` — FileCheck passes (INT8_SYM and INT8_ASYM with offset)
- [x] `ptoas --pto-arch=a5 test/basic/tquant.pto` — FileCheck passes
- [x] `ptoas --pto-arch=(a3/a5) test/basic/tqaunt_verify_invalid.pto` outputs:
```bash
'pto.tquant' op INT8_ASYM quantization requires an offset operand
'pto.tquant' op INT8_SYM quantization must not have an offset operand
'pto.tquant' op expects dst element type i8 for INT8_SYM quantization
'pto.tquant' op expects dst element type ui8 for INT8_ASYM quantization
```
- [x] Generated C++ compiles with bisheng against pto-isa headers (`dav-2201`)
- [x] End-to-end numerical accuracy verified on A3 hardware (INT8_SYM) via `test/basic/tquant_e2e.pto`:

| src | scale | expected i8 | result |
|-----|-------|-------------|--------|
| 4.0 | 0.25 | 1 | ✅ |
| 2.0 | 0.50 | 1 | ✅ |
| 10.0 | 1.00 | 10 | ✅ |
| 127.0 | 1.00 | 127 | ✅ |
| -4.0 | 0.25 | -1 | ✅ |
| 0.0 | 1.00 | 0 | ✅ |

- [x] End-to-end numerical accuracy verified on A3 hardware (INT8_ASYM) via `test/basic/tquant_e2e.pto`:

| src | scale | offset | expected ui8 | result |
|-----|-------|--------|--------------|--------|
| 4.0 | 0.25 | 0.0 | 1 | ✅ |
| 10.0 | 1.00 | 0.0 | 10 | ✅ |
| 4.0 | 1.00 | 10.0 | 14 | ✅ |
| 200.0 | 1.00 | 0.0 | 200 | ✅ |
| 255.0 | 1.00 | 0.0 | 255 | ✅ |
| 0.0 | 1.00 | 0.0 | 0 | ✅ |

> **Note:** multi-function `.pto` modules require explicit `pto.entry` on each kernel — without it only a single-function module is auto-detected as an entry and gets `__global__ AICORE`. Both functions in `tquant_e2e.pto` are annotated accordingly.

To reproduce:
```bash
build/tools/ptoas/ptoas \
  --pto-arch=a3 \
  --enable-insert-sync \
  test/basic/tquant_e2e.pto \
  -o /mounted_home/tquant_e2e_a3.cpp

bisheng \
  -I/sources/pto-isa/include \
  -I/sources/pto-isa/include/pto \
  -fPIC -shared -O2 -std=c++17 \
  -xcce -Xhost-start -Xhost-end \
  --npu-arch=dav-2201 -DMEMORY_BASE \
  /mounted_home/tquant_caller.cpp \
  -o /mounted_home/tquant_e2e_a3.so

python test_quantop.py
```

Scripts used for testing:
- `tquant_caller.cpp`
```cpp
#include "tquant_e2e_a3.cpp"

extern "C" void call_tquant_sym(
    void *stream,
    uint8_t *src,
    uint8_t *fp,
    uint8_t *dst)
{
    tquant_sym_run<<<20, nullptr, stream>>>(
        reinterpret_cast<float *>(src),
        reinterpret_cast<float *>(fp),
        reinterpret_cast<int8_t *>(dst));
}

extern "C" void call_tquant_asym(
    void *stream,
    uint8_t *src,
    uint8_t *fp,
    uint8_t *offset,
    uint8_t *dst)
{
    tquant_asym_run<<<20, nullptr, stream>>>(
        reinterpret_cast<float *>(src),
        reinterpret_cast<float *>(fp),
        reinterpret_cast<float *>(offset),
        reinterpret_cast<uint8_t *>(dst));
}

```
- `test_quantop.py`
```python
import ctypes
import torch
import torch_npu

device = "npu:0"
torch.npu.set_device(device)

lib = ctypes.CDLL("/mounted_home/tquant_e2e_a3.so")

fn_sym = lib.call_tquant_sym
fn_sym.argtypes = [ctypes.c_void_p, ctypes.c_void_p, ctypes.c_void_p, ctypes.c_void_p]
fn_sym.restype = None

fn_asym = lib.call_tquant_asym
fn_asym.argtypes = [ctypes.c_void_p, ctypes.c_void_p, ctypes.c_void_p, ctypes.c_void_p, ctypes.c_void_p]
fn_asym.restype = None

def run_sym(src_val, scale_val):
    src = torch.full((32, 32), src_val, dtype=torch.float32, device=device)
    fp  = torch.full((32, 32), scale_val, dtype=torch.float32, device=device)
    dst = torch.full((32, 32), -1, dtype=torch.int8, device=device)
    stream_ptr = torch.npu.current_stream()._as_parameter_
    torch.npu.synchronize()
    fn_sym(stream_ptr,
           ctypes.c_void_p(src.data_ptr()),
           ctypes.c_void_p(fp.data_ptr()),
           ctypes.c_void_p(dst.data_ptr()))
    torch.npu.synchronize()
    return dst

def run_asym(src_val, scale_val, offset_val):
    src    = torch.full((32, 32), src_val,    dtype=torch.float32, device=device)
    fp     = torch.full((32, 32), scale_val,  dtype=torch.float32, device=device)
    offset = torch.full((32, 32), offset_val, dtype=torch.float32, device=device)
    dst    = torch.full((32, 32), 255,        dtype=torch.uint8,   device=device)
    stream_ptr = torch.npu.current_stream()._as_parameter_
    torch.npu.synchronize()
    fn_asym(stream_ptr,
            ctypes.c_void_p(src.data_ptr()),
            ctypes.c_void_p(fp.data_ptr()),
            ctypes.c_void_p(offset.data_ptr()),
            ctypes.c_void_p(dst.data_ptr()))
    torch.npu.synchronize()
    return dst

sym_cases = [
    # (src,    scale,  expected_i8)
    (4.0,    0.25,    1),    # 4.0 * 0.25 = 1.0
    (2.0,    0.5,     1),    # 2.0 * 0.5  = 1.0
    (10.0,   1.0,    10),    # 10.0 * 1.0 = 10.0
    (127.0,  1.0,   127),    # max i8
    (-4.0,   0.25,   -1),    # -4.0 * 0.25 = -1.0
    (0.0,    1.0,     0),    # zero
]

asym_cases = [
    # (src,    scale,  offset,  expected_ui8)   expected = round(src * scale + offset)
    (4.0,    0.25,    0.0,    1),    # 4.0 * 0.25 + 0.0 = 1.0
    (10.0,   1.0,     0.0,   10),    # 10.0 * 1.0 + 0.0 = 10.0
    (4.0,    1.0,    10.0,   14),    # 4.0 * 1.0 + 10.0 = 14.0
    (200.0,  1.0,     0.0,  200),    # large positive
    (255.0,  1.0,     0.0,  255),    # max ui8
    (0.0,    1.0,     0.0,    0),    # zero
]

all_passed = True

print("=== INT8_SYM ===")
for src_val, scale_val, expected in sym_cases:
    dst = run_sym(src_val, scale_val)
    ok = (dst == expected).all()
    all_passed = all_passed and ok
    print(f"src={src_val:6.1f} scale={scale_val:.2f} -> expected={expected:4d}  got={dst[0,0].item():4d}  {'PASS' if ok else 'FAIL'}")

print()
print("=== INT8_ASYM ===")
for src_val, scale_val, offset_val, expected in asym_cases:
    dst = run_asym(src_val, scale_val, offset_val)
    ok = (dst == expected).all()
    all_passed = all_passed and ok
    print(f"src={src_val:6.1f} scale={scale_val:.2f} offset={offset_val:.1f} -> expected={expected:4d}  got={dst[0,0].item():4d}  {'PASS' if ok else 'FAIL'}")

print()
print("ALL PASSED" if all_passed else "SOME TESTS FAILED")

```

items.title: Display macOS Finder color tags
items.body:
## Summary
Read the \`com.apple.metadata:_kMDItemUserTags\` xattr via \`ctypes\`-bound \`libc.getxattr\` (macOS-specific signature) and parse the binary plist with stdlib \`plistlib\`. Each tag string is \`"Name\nIndex"\` where the index maps to a Finder color (1-7); colorless tags (index 0) are skipped.

Listing entries now carry an optional \`tags\` array of color indices, and the list/column views render small colored dots after the file name (matching Finder's palette: gray, green, purple, blue, yellow, red, orange).

Zero new dependencies — pure stdlib (\`ctypes\`, \`plistlib\`).

Closes #48

- 7 new server-side tests covering parsing, multi-tag, colorless skip, and the listing endpoint (all pass)
- [ ] In Finder, tag a file with a color → reload finder-pane → colored dot appears next to the name
- [ ] Multi-tag a file with several colors → multiple dots in order

items.title: feat: add SWIG support for generating multi-language bindings
items.body:
libcoraza needs SWIG compatibility to enable connector authors to generate bindings for Python, Ruby, Java, PHP, Perl, and other languages from the existing C API.

## Changes

- **`coraza.i`** — SWIG interface file:
  - Types declared independently to avoid exposing CGO-generated Go runtime boilerplate (`_GoString_`, `GoInt`, etc.) to SWIG
  - `char **er` output parameters hidden from target language via typemaps; raises a runtime exception with the actual error message on failure (language-specific: `SWIG_JavaThrowException` for Java, `SWIG_Error` + `free` + `SWIG_fail` for others) — C error string is freed after capture to prevent memory leaks
  - `%newobject` on `coraza_matched_rule_get_error_log` so target language runtime owns and frees the returned string
  - `coraza_add_debug_log_callback` / `coraza_add_error_callback` excluded via `%ignore` — function pointer handling is inherently language-specific and must be addressed per-binding
  - Python `bytes`/`bytearray` typemap: collapses `(const unsigned char *data, int length)` into a single native bytes argument
  - Java `byte[]` typemap: same pair mapped to a single `byte[]` argument
  - Java JNI library-load pragma: `System.loadLibrary("coraza_jni")` automatically embedded in the generated `corazaJNI` class
  - Source-of-truth comment cross-referencing `libcoraza/coraza.go` and `make check-swig-sync`

- **`libcoraza/coraza.go`** — Sync warning comment added before the first `//export` annotation, directing developers to update `coraza.i` and run `make check-swig-sync` when adding, removing, or changing exported functions

- **`configure.ac`** — Optional SWIG detection via `AC_PATH_PROG` + `AM_CONDITIONAL([HAVE_SWIG])`; shown in configure summary

- **`Makefile.am`** — Conditional `swig` target (present only when SWIG is found); target language and output directory configurable via `SWIG_LANG` (default: `python`) and `SWIG_OUTDIR` (default: `.`):
  ```sh
  make swig                                          # generates Python bindings
  make swig SWIG_LANG=ruby                           # generates Ruby bindings
  make swig SWIG_LANG=java SWIG_OUTDIR=examples/java/gen  # generates Java bindings
  ```
  New `check-swig-sync` target verifies every `//export` function in `libcoraza/coraza.go` is accounted for in `coraza.i` (as an `extern` declaration or `%ignore`); exits non-zero if any are missing — safe to run in CI.

- **`examples/python/`** — Self-contained Python SWIG example directory:
  - `simple_get.py` — covers all exported functions testable without a native callback, including `coraza_append_request_body` / `coraza_append_response_body` via the native bytes typemap
  - `Makefile` — `make` builds the SWIG extension; `make run` executes the example; `make clean` removes generated files
  - `README.md` — prerequisites, build/run instructions, and function coverage table

- **`examples/java/`** — Self-contained Java SWIG example directory:
  - `SimpleGet.java` — covers all exported functions testable without a native callback using `byte[]` for body functions
  - `Makefile` — `make` generates the JNI library and compiles Java classes; `make run` executes the example; `make clean` removes generated artifacts
  - `README.md` — prerequisites, build/run instructions, and function coverage table

- **`.github/workflows/swig.yml`** — CI workflow with two jobs (`swig-python`, `swig-java`), each tested against Go 1.24.x and 1.25.x on Ubuntu: installs dependencies, builds libcoraza, then delegates to `make -C examples/python run` / `make -C examples/java run`; all actions pinned to latest release SHA

- **`.github/workflows/make-install.yml`** — Updated to pin all actions to latest release SHA

- **`README.md`** — Documents SWIG prerequisites, build steps, and a complete Python compilation example; also fixes pre-existing `$LID_LIBRARY_PATH` typo

- **`.gitignore`** — Excludes generated SWIG wrapper sources, language modules, `examples/java/gen/`, and other per-directory build artifacts; tracks `examples/python/Makefile` and `examples/java/Makefile` via negation rules

All GitHub Actions across all workflows are pinned by full commit SHA alongside a version comment (e.g. `actions/setup-go@<sha> # v6.3.0`, `actions/checkout@<sha> # v6.0.2`) for supply-chain security.

items.title: odb: fix Python == and hash for db* proxy objects
items.body:
Fixes #5960.

## Problem

SWIG creates a new Python proxy object every time a C++ pointer is returned, so two handles to the same underlying object obtained via different code paths had different proxy addresses. Python's default `==` compares identity, causing it to always return `False`:

```python
iterm1 = inst.findITerm("a")
iterm2 = net.getITerms()[0]
iterm1 == iterm2  # False — wrong, both wrap the same C++ object
```

This also made `hash()` inconsistent, breaking use of db objects in sets and dicts.

## Root cause

`dbObject.h` was only processed by SWIG transitively (via `#include` inside `db.h`, not via `%include`). SWIG therefore never registered `dbObject`'s public methods when generating derived class wrappers, so `getId()` and `getObjectType()` were absent from all `db*` Python classes and `dbObject` itself was not in their MRO.

## Fix

**`src/odb/src/swig/common/odb.i`**
- `%include "odb/dbObject.h"` directly before `%include "odb/db.h"` so `dbObject` becomes a proper Python base class and all derived classes (`dbNet`, `dbITerm`, etc.) inherit `getId()` and `getObjectType()` through normal Python MRO.
- `%ignore` two methods that cannot be wrapped at this point: `getImpl()` (not exported from the library) and `getType(const char*, utl::Logger*)` (`utl::Logger` is not yet in SWIG scope).
- `#ifdef SWIGPYTHON %include "dboperators.i" #endif` at the end so the Python patch runs after all classes are defined.

**`src/odb/src/swig/python/dboperators.i`**
- `%pythoncode` block that installs `__eq__`, `__ne__`, and `__hash__` on all `dbObject`-derived classes at module import time.
- `__eq__` uses `self.this == other.this` (SwigPyObject pointer comparison — the correct semantic for database handles).
- `__hash__` uses `hash((getObjectType(), getId()))` — a stable, portable key consistent with pointer equality.
- `%pythoncode` is used instead of `%extend` to avoid SWIG Warning 303, which is fatal under `-Werror` in SWIG >= 4.3.

**`src/odb/test/test_iterm.py`**
- New `test_equality` method reproduces the bug and verifies `==`, `!=`, `hash()`, set deduplication, and dict keying for handles obtained via different paths.

## Testing

```
ctest -R '^odb\.' --output-on-failure
```

All odb Python unit tests pass. Tcl tests are unaffected (`#ifdef SWIGPYTHON` ensures the patch is Python-only).

AI tools were used to assist with code generation. All technical decisions and implementation details are my own.

items.title: Add ctypes C-ABI error bridging to prevent worker crashes during kern…
items.body:
…el tuning

AITER_CHECK and HIP_CALL now throw std::runtime_error instead of calling std::terminate()/exit(0), so exceptions can be caught at the C-ABI boundary.

New header aiter_ctypes_error.h provides:
- AITER_CTYPES_ERROR_DEF: per-TU thread-local error storage + ABI version probe
- AITER_CTYPES_DEFINE_ENTRYPOINT_VOID: macro that generates extern "C" int wrapper with automatic try/catch bridging (developer writes normal function body)
- aiter_safe_call: template that catches C++ exceptions, stores in TLS, returns -1

Python side (core.py) probes each .so for aiter_ctypes_abi_version to auto-detect the new int-returning convention and raises RuntimeError on failure.

This is mainly for tuning, not terminating the process when there is checking error.

items.title: アクセシビリティチェックの PyInstaller bundle 内 segfault を修正
items.body:
## 概要
- `AXIsProcessTrustedWithOptions` を ctypes 直呼び出しから pyobjc の `ApplicationServices` バインディングに置き換え
- 旧 ctypes コードは PyInstaller bundle 内で `CFGetTypeID`（faulting address 0x8）で確実に segfault していた。`CFDictionaryCreate` が NULL を返しており、原因は手動 lookup した `kCFBooleanTrue` のポインタがフレームワークインスタンス越しで解決されないこと
- PyInstaller の hidden imports に `ApplicationServices` / `HIServices` を追加して bundle に含める
- テストは新しい実装に合わせて書き直し

## レビュー反映
- `sys.platform != "darwin"` を早期 return に追加（ImportError ≠ 非 macOS の誤判定を排除）
- macOS 上で pyobjc の ImportError が起きた場合は warning を出して False を返す（install/bundling バグを黙らせない）
- 対応するテストを追加

## 再現・検証
- Before: `open -n dist/VoiceInput.app` で即座に `EXC_BAD_ACCESS` クラッシュ（`~/Library/Logs/DiagnosticReports/` に crash report）
- After: アクセシビリティ設定に応じて True/False を正しく返す

## テスト方針
- [x] `uv run pytest tests/` (全件 pass)
- [ ] 再ビルドした bundle を `open dist/VoiceInput.app` で起動、segfault しないことを確認
- [ ] メニューバーアイコンが表示されることを確認

items.body:
feat(nvdsinfer): improve TensorRT engine file naming and auto-discovery

Problem:
- Engine filenames only included batch size, ignoring input dimensions,
  GPU model, and TensorRT version
- System only looked for engines via model-engine-file config parameter,
  making it effectively obsolete
- Valid existing engines were ignored, causing unnecessary rebuilds
- Changing input size caused infinite rebuild cycles (files overwritten)

Solution:
- New naming format: {model}_b{batch}_i{WxH}_{sm}__{gpu}_{trt}_{precision}.engine
  Example: model_b8_i640x640_sm86_rtx3090_trt10.7_fp16.engine
- Implement 3-step engine discovery: config path → standardized path → rebuild
- Auto-discover existing engines without requiring model-engine-file param
- Include GPU compute capability AND model name for hardware-specific optimization
- Add TensorRT version to prevent cross-version incompatibility
- Automatic fallback to rebuild on any failure (corrupted file, param mismatch)

Benefits:
- Reduces pipeline startup from minutes to seconds when engine exists
- Each configuration gets its own engine file (no overwrites)
- Works across different GPUs with proper identification
- Self-healing: auto-rebuilds on any failure
- Backward compatible: model-engine-file still works if specified

Files modified:
- nvdsinfer_model_builder.h: Add helper function declarations
- nvdsinfer_model_builder.cpp: Implement naming logic and helpers
- nvdsinfer_context_impl.cpp: Implement 3-step engine discovery
- ENGINE_FILE_NAMING_IMPROVEMENT.md: Documentation

## Summary

Add free-threaded Python (PEP 703 / nogil) support for fastdb4py, bumping version to **0.1.15**.

## Changes

### Build System
- `pyproject.toml`: require `swig>=4.4` (was `>=4.0`); add `cibuildwheel` free-threaded support
- `setup.py`: auto-detect `Py_GIL_DISABLED` → pass to CMake
- `CMakeLists.txt`: conditionally pass `-nogil` to SWIG when building on free-threaded Python
- CI: add Python 3.13t to test matrix (`continue-on-error`)

### SWIG Interface
- `%feature("threadallow")` for 6 pure C++ functions (GIL release)
- `Py_BEGIN_ALLOW_THREADS` around `copy_to_buffer` memcpy
- SWIG 4.4 `-nogil` flag auto-declares `Py_MOD_GIL_NOT_USED`

### Python Thread Safety
- `_schema.py`: `WeakKeyDictionary` reads fully under lock
- `serializer.py`: `_CLASS_SCHEMA_CACHE` reads fully under lock
- `table.py`: per-instance `_cache_lock` on `ColumnAccessor`
- `feature.py`: `_cache` eager `{}` init (eliminates `_get_cache()` race)

### Tests
- 12 new concurrent thread-safety tests in `test_free_threading.py`

### Docs
- README: "Free-threaded Python" section with thread-safety table
- CHANGELOG: entries in `fastdb4py` and `fastdb-core` sections

## Test Results
111 passed, 0 failed (99 existing + 12 new)

items.title: Fix ICM recursion/chip transfer, add range-aware equity, hybrid GTO wiring, opponent targeting, plus WindowActivator hardening
items.body:
### Motivation
- Correct mathematically incorrect ICM recursion and chip-flow on call branches which produced wrong tournament EVs.
- Make equity estimates and decision logic range-aware instead of sampling uniform-random opponent hands.
- Wire hybrid/GTO second-opinion into the decision context in an advisory/traceable way so hybrid mode reflects real consults.
- Improve primary-opponent selection and relative-position handling and harden execution layer init on non-Windows CI/dev hosts.

### Description
- Fixed ICM recursion by carrying full branch probability into deeper finishing positions and updated `icm_call_ev()` to transfer chips to/from a targeted opponent instead of only mutating hero stack, and added `opponent_index` support in `TournamentLogic`.  
- Replaced uniform opponent sampling with a coverage-filtered opponent pool in `EquityEngine.estimate_equity_vs_range()` and added `_filtered_opponent_pool()` and a preflop combo-ranking helper; equity results are cached per `(hero, board, coverage)`.  
- Activated hybrid/GTO advisory wiring at context-build time: `DecisionContext.gto_opinion` is populated when hybrid consults are enabled and `consult_gto()` is invoked defensively; added `_hybrid_gto_consult_enabled()`.  
- Improved decision targeting and relative-position logic in `DecisionEngine`: ` _primary_opponent_seat()` now prefers last non-hero aggressor then last money-in actor with an ordered-seat fallback; `relative_position` is computed and used to derive `position_advantage`.  
- Introduced table-driven preflop RFI thresholds via `_preflop_rfi_threshold()` and tightened/shifted `bubble_pressure` gating and hybrid open ordering; added `_icm_win_probability()` to prefer measured `real_equity` and otherwise adjust by `opponent_range.coverage` and multiway penalty.  
- Added structured range line adjustments: `RangeNarrower._LINE_ADJUSTMENTS` plus `line_adjustments()` are applied to opponent ranges and used to modulate bluff gates via `_line_bluff_modifier()`.  
- Hardened `WindowActivator` initialization to not assume `ctypes.windll` exists by using `getattr(ctypes, "windll", None)` so non-Windows imports/tests do not crash.  
- Tests: added/updated unit tests to cover ICM behavior, equity filtering, range line adjustments, primary-opponent selection, hybrid GTO population, preflop RFI thresholds, and the WindowActivator non-Windows path; relevant test files are `tests/test_tournament_logic.py`, `tests/test_equity_engine.py`, `tests/test_range_model.py`, `tests/test_window_activator.py`, and updates in `tests/test_decision_engine.py`.

### Testing
- Ran the unit test suite with `pytest`; all new and updated tests passed.  
- Verified `tests/test_tournament_logic.py` asserts equal-stack sanity and opponent-transfer semantics and passes.  
- Verified `tests/test_equity_engine.py` and `tests/test_range_model.py` assert coverage-sensitive equity and line-adjustment behavior and pass.  
- Verified `tests/test_window_activator.py` covers missing `ctypes.windll` initialization and passes.

------

items.title: Add f32 namespace to navAggregate
items.body:
* **Tickets addressed:** ema-gnc-2130
* **Review:** By commit  <!-- Choose from: "by commit", "by file" -->
* **Merge strategy:** Merge (no squash)  <!-- Choose from: "merge (no squash)", "squash and merge" -->

## Description
F32 (single-precision) modules under src/fp32-modules/algorithms/ define C++ types with identical names to their non-F32 counterparts in src/fswAlgorithms/. When both are loaded in the same Python process (e.g., pytest from root), SWIG's global type table — keyed by C++ type name — collides. The last module to swigregister wins the clientdata (Python class), causing property getters on shared types like AggregateAttInput to wrap members with the wrong ReadFunctor type.

The solution is to namespace modules (namespaces throughout xmera is also part of the solution but not something we can do immediately). 

The following are the changes made to accommodate module namespacing
  - Headers (.h): wrapped types in namespace f32 {}
  - Implementations (.cpp): added using namespace f32;
  - C wrappers (_c.cpp): explicit ::f32:: qualification in casts
  - SWIG (.i): using namespace f32; in %{}, %rename directives, qualified %attribute/STRUCTASLIST
  - C++ tests: using namespace f32; in helpers/test files
  - Python tests: no changes needed


## Verification
All test runs as expected.

## Documentation
NA

## Future work
None

items.title: Add INVARIANT-5/6/7 input validation, secret key zeroing, and constant-time fallback warnings
items.body: ## Pull Request Description ### Summary This PR implements three critical security invariants for the AMA Cryptography library: 1. **INVARIANT-5**: Input validation at the Python/C boundary for all fixed-size buffers and fixed-width integer parameters before ctypes dispatch 2. **INVARIANT-6**: Secret key zeroing via mutable `bytearray` storage and `wipe()` methods on all PQC key-pair dataclasses 3. **INVARIANT-7**: Observable fallback warnings when native constant-time C backends are unavailable, with `AMA_REQUIRE_CONSTANT_TIME` enforcement Additionally, adds HMAC-SHA-512 native support for BIP32 key derivation and updates architectural documentation. ### Type of Change - [x] Security fix (addresses security vulnerabilities) - [x] New feature (HMAC-SHA-512 native backend) - [x] Code refactoring (improves code quality without changing functionality) - [x] Test enhancement (adds comprehensive negative-path tests) - [x] Documentation update (INVARIANTS.md, CMakeLists.txt compiler requirements) ### Related Issues Relates to architectural security invariants documented in `.github/INVARIANTS.md` ## Motivation and Context ### Why is this change required? The library previously lacked: - **Input validation before ctypes dispatch**: Malformed fixed-size buffers (keys, public keys) could cause buffer overflows or undefined behavior in the native C library - **Secret key zeroing guarantees**: PQC secret keys stored as immutable `bytes` could not be securely wiped from memory, violating cryptographic best practices - **Observable fallback behavior**: Silent fallback to pure-Python (non-constant-time) implementations could mask timing side-channel vulnerabilities in production ### What problem does it solve? 1. **Buffer overflow prevention**: Validates all fixed-size buffer lengths (Dilithium/Kyber/SPHINCS keys, Argon2id parameters) before C dispatch 2. **Memory safety**: Ensures secret key material is zeroed on all exit paths (normal return, exception, garbage collection) 3. **Timing side-channel visibility**: Logs warnings when constant-time guarantees are lost; allows production deployments to enforce native-only execution via `AMA_REQUIRE_CONSTANT_TIME=true` ## Cryptographic Impact ### Security Impact Assessment - [x] Modifies existing cryptographic operation (requires security analysis) - [x] Changes key management approach - [x] Affects multiple security layers ### Standards Compliance - [x] RFC 2104 (HMAC) — adds HMAC-SHA-512 for BIP32 - [x] NIST FIPS 204 (Dilithium) - [x] NIST FIPS 203 (Kyber) - [x] NIST SP 800-108 (Key Derivation) ### Security Analysis **Classical Security:** - Input validation prevents buffer overflows and integer wrapping attacks on fixed-width parameters (e.g., Argon2id `t_cost` > 2^32-1) - Secret key zeroing eliminates memory disclosure vulnerabilities from unwiped heap allocations **Quantum Security:** - No impact on quantum resistance of PQC algorithms themselves - Improves operational security of key material handling **Security Impact:** - **Maintains and improves** current security posture by closing key management and input validation gaps - Prevents silent degradation to non-constant-time implementations ## Implementation Details ### Changes Made 1. **Input Validation (INVARIANT-5)**: - Added length checks for all fixed-size buffers in `dilithium_sign()`, `dilithium_verify()`, `dilithium_verify_ctx()`, `kyber_encapsulate()`, `kyber_decapsulate()`, `sphincs_sign()`, `sphincs_verify()`, `sphincs_verify_ctx()` - Added range validation for Argon2id parameters (`t_cost`, `m_cost`, `parallelism`) against `c_uint32` bounds - Raises `ValueError` with descript https://claude.ai/code/session_01YLeHAWfBwK6mDRttB8Z41B ---

items.title: fix: silence Qt DPI awareness warning on Windows
items.body:
Qt 6 calls `SetProcessDpiAwarenessContext(DPI_AWARENESS_CONTEXT_PER_MONITOR_AWARE_V2)` during `QApplication` init. The manual `ctypes.windll.shcore.SetProcessDpiAwareness(2)` call at module load was racing it — Windows only allows setting DPI awareness once per process, so Qt's attempt returned `E_ACCESSDENIED` and printed the warning to stderr.

## Changes

- **Removed** the `ctypes` DPI awareness block from module startup — Qt 6 sets the newer `DPI_AWARENESS_CONTEXT_PER_MONITOR_AWARE_V2` context automatically, which is the functional superset of the removed `PROCESS_PER_MONITOR_DPI_AWARE` (value 2) call
- **Updated** the module docstring to reflect that PyQt6 now owns DPI awareness setup; `mss` and `GetCursorPos` coordinates remain physical pixels since `QApplication` is constructed before any capture occurs

```python
# Before — conflicts with Qt 6's own SetProcessDpiAwarenessContext() call
if sys.platform == "win32":
    try:
        import ctypes
        ctypes.windll.shcore.SetProcessDpiAwareness(2)
    except Exception:
        pass

# After — removed; Qt 6 handles this during QApplication.__init__
```

items.title: Add PJSUA2 AudioMediaAiPort wrapper for AI media port
items.body:
## Summary

Wraps the experimental `pjmedia_ai_port` (from PR #4866) as a PJSUA2 `AudioMedia` subclass, enabling C++/Java/Python/C# applications to use the AI media port through the high-level API.

Ref: #4523

## Usage

```cpp
AudioMediaAiPort ai;
ai.createPort();
ai.connect("wss://api.openai.com/v1/realtime?model=...", apiKey);

// Route call audio to/from AI
callMedia.startTransmit(ai);
ai.startTransmit(callMedia);

// Later
ai.disconnect();
```

Events (connected, transcript, etc.) are delivered via the virtual `onEvent()` callback, which can be overridden in C++ or target languages via SWIG directors.

## Design

- Uses **pjsua's SIP endpoint ioqueue and timer heap** for WebSocket I/O — no separate worker thread, pjsua's existing worker threads handle polling
- Uses `pjmedia_ai_event_type` enum directly (no wrapper enum)
- Destructor nulls the `user_data` back-pointer under grp_lock to prevent use-after-free from in-flight callbacks
- Wrapper pool lifetime tied to grp_lock destroy handler to prevent premature release

## Limitations

These are known limitations for reference in future improvements:
- No SSL/TLS parameter configuration (e.g. certificate verification, client certs, cipher suites). The C API supports `ssl_param` but it is not yet exposed in the PJSUA2 wrapper.
- No way to customize the OpenAI session configuration (voice, instructions, modalities) — currently hardcoded in the backend
- No `isConnected()` / `getState()` query method — connection state can only be inferred from `onEvent()` callbacks
- No JSON persistence (`readObject`/`writeObject`) for `AiMediaPortParam`

## Changes

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `pjsip/include/pjsua2/media.hpp` | `AiMediaEvent`, `AiMediaPortParam`, `AudioMediaAiPort` class |
| `pjsip/src/pjsua2/media.cpp` | Implementation (~120 lines) |
| `pjmedia/include/pjmedia/ai_port.h` | Add `pjmedia_ai_port_set_user_data()` |
| `pjmedia/src/pjmedia/ai_port.c` | Implement `set_user_data()` |
| `pjmedia/include/pjmedia.h` | Add `ai_port.h` to umbrella header |
| `pjsip-apps/src/swig/pjsua2.i` | Add director feature for `AudioMediaAiPort` |
| `pjsip-apps/src/swig/symbols.lst` | Add `pjmedia_ai_event_type` |
| `pjsip-apps/src/swig/symbols.i` | Regenerated with `importsym2.py` |
| `pjsip-apps/src/swig/python/test.py` | Python SWIG test for AI port lifecycle + optional OpenAI connection |

## Test plan
- [x] Build verified on Linux (zero warnings)
- [x] Build on Windows/MSVC (Release-Static, C++ lib clean)
- [x] Python SWIG test: create, conf bridge register, destroy (WSL)
- [x] Python SWIG test with OpenAI connection on MSYS2/MinGW (real audio, confirmed working)

## Asynchronous Background Prefetcher
A new BackgroundPrefetcher class has been implemented in gcsfs/prefetcher.py ([ source](https://github.com/fsspec/gcsfs/compare/main...ankitaluthra1:gcsfs:regional?expand=1&content_ref=class+backgroundprefetcher)). This component is designed to:

- Proactively Fetch Data: It spawns a background producer task that fetches sequential blocks of data before they are explicitly requested ([ source](https://github.com/fsspec/gcsfs/compare/main...ankitaluthra1:gcsfs:regional?expand=1&content_ref=this+reader+spawns+a+background+producer+task+that+fetches+sequential+blocks+of+data+before+they+are+explicitly+requested)).
- Adaptive Blocksize: The engine dynamically adjusts its blocksize based on the history of requested read sizes ([ source](https://github.com/fsspec/gcsfs/compare/main...ankitaluthra1:gcsfs:regional?expand=1&content_ref=returns+the+adaptive+blocksize+configuration)).
- Sequential Streak Detection: Prefetching is triggered after detecting a "streak" of sequential reads ([ source](https://github.com/fsspec/gcsfs/compare/main...ankitaluthra1:gcsfs:regional?expand=1&content_ref=self+sequential_streak+0)).
- Optimized Slicing: Uses ctypes for a fast, low-overhead slice implementation (_fast_slice) to manage internal buffers ([ source](https://github.com/fsspec/gcsfs/compare/main...ankitaluthra1:gcsfs:regional?expand=1&content_ref=pybytes_fromstringandsize+ctypes+pythonapi+pybytes_fromstringandsize)).


## Core Refactoring for Concurrency
The file-fetching logic in gcsfs/core.py has been refactored to enable parallel downloads:

- _cat_file Decomposition: _cat_file is now split into _cat_file_sequential and _cat_file_concurrent ([ source](https://github.com/fsspec/gcsfs/compare/main...ankitaluthra1:gcsfs:regional?expand=1&content_ref=async+def+_cat_file_sequential+self+path+start+none+end+none+kwargs)).
- Threshold-Based Routing: Concurrent fetching is automatically utilized when the requested data size exceeds MIN_CHUNK_SIZE_FOR_CONCURRENCY (defaulting to 5MB) and multiple concurrency slots are requested ([ source](https://github.com/fsspec/gcsfs/compare/main...ankitaluthra1:gcsfs:regional?expand=1&content_ref=min_chunk_size_for_concurrency+5+1024+1024)).
- Integration: The GCSFile object now optionally initializes the _prefetch_engine when the use_prefetch_reader flag is provided ([ source](https://github.com/fsspec/gcsfs/compare/main...ankitaluthra1:gcsfs:regional?expand=1&content_ref=use_prefetch_reader+kwargs+get+use_prefetch_reader+false)).

items.title: Add Windows support (curses, PID check, clipboard, CI)
items.body:
## What
Full Windows compatibility so teammates on Windows can use copsearch.

### Changes
| Area | macOS/Linux | Windows |
|------|------------|---------|
| **TUI** | stdlib curses | `windows-curses` (auto-installed) |
| **PID alive check** | `os.kill(pid, 0)` | `ctypes.windll.kernel32.OpenProcess` |
| **Resume** | `os.execlp` (replaces process) | `subprocess.call` (runs then exits) |
| **Clipboard** | `pbcopy` / `xclip` | `clip.exe` |

### CI
Expanded from 4 jobs to **12 jobs** (3 OS x 4 Python versions):
- ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest
- Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12, 3.13

## Summary

Major enhancement of the SS-Tools scanner into **Minecraft SS AntiCheat Scanner v1.0** with multi-layer detection and smart whitelisting.

### Changes
